content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

This log records all verses in 1 Peter Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 — "malice" vs. "evil behavior" — scope of kakian

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all malice and all deceit and hypocrisies and envies and all evil speakings" | Literal plurals preserved; "evil speakings" for katalalia |
| LOGOS | "all malice and all deceit and hypocrisy and envy and all slander" | Formal singulars; "slander" for katalalia |
| PNEUMA | "all evil behavior. Stop being deceitful, hypocritical, jealous, and talking behind each other's backs" | Dynamic paraphrase splitting into two sentences; converts nouns to adjectives/verbs |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's literal plurals ("hypocrisies," "envies") are grammatically accurate (Greek has plurals) but awkward in English. PNEUMA's paraphrase breaks the vice list structure and converts abstract nouns into behavioral descriptions, losing the formal parallel structure of Peter's rhetoric.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 — "pure spiritual milk" vs. "rational, guileless milk" — logikon adolon gala

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the rational, guileless milk" | Preserves both Greek adjectives literally; logikon as "rational," adolon as "guileless" |
| LOGOS | "the pure spiritual milk" | Renders logikon as "spiritual" and adolon as "pure" |
| PNEUMA | "the pure spiritual milk of God's word" | Adds "of God's word" to make the logos connection explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "pure spiritual milk" with LOGOS. Logikon carries a deliberate double sense ("rational/spiritual" and "pertaining to logos/word") that no single English word captures. "Spiritual" is the best approximation for a general audience. EMET's "rational" is lexically valid but misleading in English (suggesting intellectual rather than spiritual nourishment). PNEUMA's addition "of God's word" resolves the wordplay in one direction and adds words not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's lexical note on the logos/logikon wordplay is preserved in the apparatus.

---

## 2:3 — "if indeed you have tasted" vs. "if you tasted" vs. "now that you have tasted"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if you tasted that the Lord is good" | Bare conditional; aorist rendered as simple past |
| LOGOS | "if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good" | "if indeed" captures the first-class conditional (assumed true) |
| PNEUMA | "now that you have tasted for yourselves that the Lord is good" | Converts conditional to declarative; adds "for yourselves" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "if indeed you have tasted." The first-class conditional (ei + indicative) assumes the condition is true ("if, as indeed you have"), and "if indeed" conveys this nuance. EMET's bare "if" sounds doubtful. PNEUMA's "now that" eliminates the conditional form entirely, which is over-interpretation even if the sense is correct.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:4 — "by humans" vs. "by men" vs. "by people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been rejected indeed by humans" | anthropon rendered as "humans" |
| LOGOS | "rejected by men" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "rejected by people" | Gender-inclusive rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "rejected by people." Anthropon is generic, not gender-specific. "People" is accurate and avoids the ambiguity of "men" in modern English. This follows PNEUMA's rendering on this specific point.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of SOPHIA's own rendering (closer to PNEUMA). LOGOS's "men" is traditional but unnecessarily gender-specific.

---

## 2:5 — "Yeshua Christos" vs. "Jesus Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Yeshua Christos" | Retains Hebrew/Greek name forms throughout |
| LOGOS | "Jesus Christ" | Standard anglicized forms |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus Christ" | Standard anglicized forms |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Jesus Christ" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. Anglicized name forms are standard for an English translation. EMET's transliterated forms serve a scholarly purpose but impede general readability.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Consistent with the project-wide policy on name forms.

---

## 2:6 — "Sion" vs. "Zion"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sion" | Follows Sinaiticus spelling (Σιων) |
| LOGOS | "Zion" | Standard English transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "Zion" | Standard English transliteration |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Zion" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. While Sinaiticus writes Σιων, "Zion" is the established English form familiar to readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:7 — "the honor" vs. "the most important stone of all" — rendering of he time

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to you, the ones believing, is the honor" | Literal; he time as "the honor" — the preciousness/value belongs to believers |
| LOGOS | "To you therefore who believe belongs the honor" | Formal rendering with smoother syntax |
| PNEUMA | "So this stone is precious to you who believe" | Interprets "honor" as the preciousness of the stone |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek he time ("the honor/preciousness") is ambiguous — it could mean "the honor belongs to you" or "the preciousness [of the stone] is for you." LOGOS preserves the ambiguity. PNEUMA resolves it toward the stone interpretation, which is one valid reading but not the only one.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 — "to which they were also appointed" — interpretive range

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to which also they were appointed" | Literal; preserves the terse Greek without specifying agent or scope |
| LOGOS | "to which they were also appointed" | Formal; same content, slightly different word order |
| PNEUMA | "which is exactly what God destined for them" | Dynamic; names God as agent, uses "destined" for etethesan |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek eis ho kai etethesan is deliberately terse and theologically difficult. PNEUMA's expansion names God as agent (not explicit in the Greek) and uses the theologically loaded "destined" (which imports predestination debates). The consensus preserves the original terseness, allowing readers to wrestle with the text as the original audience did.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretation is defensible but too specific.

---

## 2:9 — "a people for his own possession" vs. "God's own special possession"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a people for his possession" | Literal rendering of laos eis peripoiesin |
| LOGOS | "a people for his own possession" | Adds "own" for emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "God's own special possession" | Dynamic; drops "a people" and adds "special" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The phrase echoes Exodus 19:5 LXX (laos periousios), and "a people for his own possession" maintains the covenantal formula. PNEUMA's rendering loses "a people" (laos), which is theologically significant — Peter is declaring that the church is now constituted as a people.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:9 — "excellencies" vs. "wonderful things" — aretas

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the excellencies of the one having called you" | Literal; aretas as "excellencies" |
| LOGOS | "the excellencies of him who called you" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the wonderful things he has done" | Dynamic; renders aretas as "wonderful things he has done" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "excellencies." Aretas means "virtues, excellencies, mighty deeds" — a rich term that includes both God's character and his acts. PNEUMA's "wonderful things he has done" narrows the meaning to acts alone, losing the character dimension.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 — "desires of the flesh" vs. "fleshly desires" vs. "desires of your sinful nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fleshly desires" | Literal; sarkikon as adjective modifying desires |
| LOGOS | "the passions of the flesh" | Formal; uses "passions" for epithumion |
| PNEUMA | "the desires of your sinful nature" | Dynamic; interprets sarx as "sinful nature" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the desires of the flesh" — a SOPHIA blend. "Desires" (EMET) is more accurate than "passions" (LOGOS) since epithumia is not necessarily passionate in the emotional sense. "Of the flesh" maintains the Petrine language without PNEUMA's interpretive "sinful nature," which imports a Pauline theological framework.

**Status:** Resolved with SOPHIA's own rendering, blending EMET's "desires" with a genitive construction.

---

## 2:11 — "sojourners and strangers" vs. "sojourners and exiles" vs. "foreigners and temporary residents"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sojourners and strangers" | Literal; paroikous kai parepidamous |
| LOGOS | "sojourners and exiles" | Formal; "exiles" for parepidamous |
| PNEUMA | "foreigners and temporary residents in this world" | Dynamic; adds "in this world" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "sojourners and exiles." Both terms convey alien status. "Exiles" (LOGOS) is stronger than "strangers" (EMET) and captures the sense of displacement. PNEUMA's addition "in this world" is interpretive — the text may refer to literal social status (diaspora Christians) as much as to spiritual pilgrimage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:12 — Sentence structure and "day of visitation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having the conduct of you among the nations good, that in what they speak against you as evildoers, from the good works, observing them, they may glorify the God in the day of visitation" | Literal; preserves the complex Greek participial chain |
| LOGOS | "Keep your conduct among the nations honorable, so that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may see your good works and glorify God on the day of visitation" | Formal; restructures into clear English clauses |
| PNEUMA | "Live such good lives among those who do not believe that even though they accuse you of doing wrong, they will see your honorable actions and give glory to God on the day he visits us" | Dynamic; "nations" becomes "those who do not believe"; "day of visitation" becomes "day he visits us" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's literal version is nearly unreadable in English. PNEUMA's "those who do not believe" narrows "nations" (ethne), which in Peter's context means the surrounding Gentile society, not simply unbelievers. PNEUMA's "day he visits us" resolves the ambiguity of episkope (which could be judgment or mercy) into a personal visit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "every human institution" vs. "every human creation" vs. "every human authority"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every human creation" | Literal; ktisei as "creation" |
| LOGOS | "every human institution" | Formal; interprets ktisei in political context |
| PNEUMA | "every human authority" | Dynamic; further narrows to "authority" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "every human institution" with LOGOS. Ktisis can mean "creation," "creature," or "institution/ordinance." In context (emperor, governors), "institution" is the best fit. EMET's "creation" is too broad; PNEUMA's "authority" is too narrow (institutions include offices, not just the authority exercised).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:17 — "king" vs. "emperor" — basilea

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the king" | Literal; basileus = king |
| LOGOS | "the emperor" | Contextual; in Roman context, basileus refers to the emperor |
| PNEUMA | "the emperor" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the emperor" with LOGOS/PNEUMA. While basileus literally means "king," in the first-century Roman context it designated the emperor. Peter's original audience would have understood this as a reference to the Roman emperor (likely Nero). Historical clarity takes precedence over the literal word.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:18 — "household servants" vs. "servants" — oiketai

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The ones who are household servants" | Literal; wordy but specifies the domestic context |
| LOGOS | "Servants" | Simple; generic term |
| PNEUMA | "Household servants" | Concise but preserves the domestic specificity of oiketai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Household servants" with PNEUMA. Oiketai specifically denotes domestic servants (from oikos, "house"), distinct from the broader douloi ("slaves/servants"). The domestic context matters for the household code (Haustafeln) structure of the passage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA. LOGOS's generic "Servants" loses the household specificity.

---

## 2:18 — "fear" vs. "respect" — phobos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in all fear" | Literal; phobos = fear |
| LOGOS | "with all fear" | Formal; retains "fear" |
| PNEUMA | "with all respect" | Dynamic; interprets phobos as respectful deference |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with all respect" with PNEUMA. In this context, phobos denotes respectful deference toward masters, not terror. Modern English "fear" carries connotations of fright that would distort Peter's meaning. The same word is used of "fearing God" (v.17), where reverence is clearly the sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA. EMET/LOGOS's literal "fear" is misleading in modern English for this specific context.

---

## 2:19 — "conscience of God" vs. "consciousness of God" vs. "awareness of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "conscience of God" | Literal; suneidestin theou |
| LOGOS | "consciousness of God" | Formal; slightly shifts from "conscience" to broader "consciousness" |
| PNEUMA | "because you are aware of his will" | Dynamic; interprets the phrase as awareness of God's will |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "awareness of God" — a SOPHIA blend. Suneidesis theou is an unusual phrase meaning a God-directed awareness or conscience. EMET's "conscience of God" is ambiguous in English (God's conscience?). LOGOS's "consciousness of God" is better but still unusual. PNEUMA's "aware of his will" adds "will" interpretively. "Awareness of God" captures the God-ward orientation cleanly.

**Status:** Resolved with SOPHIA's own rendering.

---

## 2:20 — "fame" vs. "credit" vs. "credit" — kleos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what kind of fame is it" | Literal; kleos = fame/glory/renown |
| LOGOS | "what credit is it" | Formal; "credit" for kleos in this rhetorical context |
| PNEUMA | "what credit is there" | Dynamic; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "credit" with LOGOS/PNEUMA. While kleos literally means "fame/renown," in this rhetorical question the sense is "what merit/credit." "Fame" (EMET) sounds oddly positive for a rebuke context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:21 — "pattern" vs. "example" — hupogrammon

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a pattern" | Literal; hupogrammos = a writing-copy, a pattern to trace over |
| LOGOS | "an example" | Standard formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "an example" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "an example" with LOGOS/PNEUMA. While hupogrammos literally refers to a writing template that students trace over (making EMET's "pattern" etymologically apt), "example" communicates the intended meaning in English more naturally. The tracing metaphor is noted in the apparatus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's etymological observation is valuable but "pattern" is less clear in English.

---

## 2:23 — "was giving over" vs. "kept entrusting" vs. "entrusted himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "but was giving over to the one judging righteously" | Literal; paredidou in imperfect tense = ongoing action; does not specify what was given over |
| LOGOS | "but kept entrusting himself to the one who judges justly" | Formal; adds "himself" as implied object; "kept entrusting" captures the imperfect |
| PNEUMA | "he entrusted himself to the one who judges justly" | Dynamic; simple past; adds "himself" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The imperfect paredidou ("was handing over") suggests continuous or repeated action — "kept entrusting" captures this well. The implied object is reflexive (himself, his cause), which LOGOS and PNEUMA rightly supply. EMET's omission of the implied object makes the sentence incomplete in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:24 — "upon the tree" vs. "on the cross" — epi to xulon

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon the tree" | Literal; xulon = wood/tree |
| LOGOS | "upon the tree" | Formal; retains xulon as "tree" |
| PNEUMA | "on the cross" | Dynamic; renders xulon as "cross" for immediate clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "upon the tree" with EMET/LOGOS. Peter's word choice (xulon, "wood/tree," not stauros, "cross") is deliberate, evoking Deuteronomy 21:23 ("cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree"). This OT allusion is theologically central to Peter's argument about Christ bearing the curse. PNEUMA's "cross" substitutes a different Greek word and loses this crucial intertextual connection.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. This is a significant theological rendering where the literal word must be preserved.

---

## 2:24 — "wound" vs. "wounds" — molopi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by the wound of whom you were healed" | Literal; molops is singular in the Greek (following Isaiah 53:5 LXX) |
| LOGOS | "By his wounds you were healed" | Formal; uses the traditional English plural "wounds" |
| PNEUMA | "By his wounds you have been healed" | Dynamic; same plural; changes tense to "have been healed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's plural "wounds" — following traditional English convention and the rhetorical expectation of the Isaiah 53:5 allusion. The singular molopi in the Greek is a collective singular (referring to the totality of Christ's suffering, not a single wound), so the English plural is not inaccurate. EMET's singular is technically precise but sounds odd in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's observation about the Greek singular is noted.

---

## 2:25 — "Overseer" vs. "Guardian" — episkopon

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Overseer" | Literal; episkopos = one who watches over |
| LOGOS | "Overseer" | Formal; same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Guardian" | Dynamic; avoids ecclesiastical connotations of "bishop/overseer" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Overseer" with EMET/LOGOS. This is the only place in the NT where episkopos is applied directly to Christ. The term connects Christ to the later church office of episkopos/bishop -- the theological point is that Christ is the true Overseer of whom all church overseers are representatives. PNEUMA's "Guardian" captures the protective sense but severs the connection to ecclesial terminology.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:25 — "turned back" vs. "returned" vs. "returned" — epestraphete

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you turned back now" | Literal; epistrepho = to turn back/return |
| LOGOS | "you have now returned" | Formal; perfect sense |
| PNEUMA | "now you have returned" | Dynamic; same meaning, different word order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "you have now returned." The aorist epestraphete indicates a completed action of conversion/return. "Returned" is clearer than EMET's "turned back," which could imply regression.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET too literal / wooden for English | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS or SOPHIA blend |
| PNEUMA too expansive / interpretive | 10 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS or EMET |
| LOGOS adopted as baseline | 15 | LOGOS rendering adopted as consensus |
| SOPHIA crafted independent rendering | 3 | Blended from multiple translators (vv.11, 18, 19) |
| PNEUMA's rendering preferred | 2 | Adopted for vv.18a (oiketai) and 18b (phobos) |
| Transliteration divergence (name forms, place names) | 2 | Anglicized forms adopted |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

1 Peter 2 reveals the characteristic tendencies of each translator with particular clarity. EMET produces wooden but lexically precise renderings that expose the Greek structure (e.g., "rational, guileless milk," "conscience of God," "the wound"). LOGOS consistently provides the most balanced formal renderings, serving as the baseline for 15 of the 25 verses. PNEUMA demonstrates both strengths and weaknesses: its rendering of oiketai as "Household servants" and phobos as "respect" (v.18) are genuinely superior to the other translators, but its expansion of terse theological phrases (e.g., "which is exactly what God destined for them" in v.8, "of God's word" in v.2, "on the cross" in v.24) consistently over-interprets the Greek.

The most theologically significant disagreements in this chapter concern: (1) the predestinarian clause in v.8, where SOPHIA preserves the original terseness against PNEUMA's expansion; (2) the rendering of xulon as "tree" vs. "cross" in v.24, where the OT allusion demands preserving the literal word; and (3) the unusual phrase suneidesis theou in v.19, where SOPHIA crafted an independent rendering ("awareness of God") that none of the three translators had offered.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-06*
